news_summary: Scientists and organizations are advancing technology with breakthroughs in particle-tracking cameras, innovative aerospace designs, inclusive AI development, and novel photoreduction techniques.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques et les organisations font progresser la technologie grâce à des avancées majeures dans le domaine des caméras de suivi de particules, des conceptions aérospatiales innovantes, du développement d'une IA inclusive et des techniques novatrices de photoréduction.
news_summary_es: Los científicos y las organizaciones están impulsando el avance tecnológico gracias a los avances en cámaras de seguimiento de partículas, diseños aeroespaciales innovadores, el desarrollo inclusivo de la inteligencia artificial y nuevas técnicas de fotorreducción.

articles.title: Scientists built a camera that can track invisible particles in 3D
articles.summary: A new particle detector called PLATON could replace millions of tiny detector components with a single block of light-producing material. Using a light-field camera, highly sensitive photon sensors, and AI, it reconstructs particle paths in fast, detailed 3D. Simulations suggest it could match or surpass today’s best detectors while being far easier to scale. The technology may also lead to sharper PET medical scans.
articles.content:
Some breakthroughs in physics come from brand new inventions. Others begin with a new theory. But many advances happen when researchers combine familiar technologies in an unexpected way and create something more powerful than the individual parts.

That strategy could be especially valuable in the search for weakly interacting particles, including neutrinos and certain dark matter candidates. These particles are notoriously difficult to detect because they rarely interact with ordinary matter. Building larger detectors and improving their spatial resolution can increase the odds of observing the faint signals they produce, but doing so often makes the instruments more complicated and expensive.

Similar demands apply to calorimeters, the devices used in collider experiments to measure the energy carried by particles.

Why Particle Detectors Are So Complex

Most particle physics experiments need to reconstruct the three-dimensional (3D) paths of elementary particles as they move through large volumes of dense material.

One common detector material is a scintillator. When a charged particle passes through a scintillator, the material gives off tiny flashes of visible light. Scientists use those flashes to determine where the particle traveled and how it interacted with the detector.

To pinpoint the particle's location, the scintillator is usually divided into a vast number of small active sections. Optical fibers collect the photons produced in each section and carry the light to photomultiplier tubes or silicon photomultipliers, which count the photons.

This approach can be highly precise, but it becomes difficult to scale.

The T2K neutrino-oscillation experiment in Japan, for example, uses a detector with about two tons of sensitive material made from approximately two million cubes and 60,000 fibers. At CERN and the Paul Scherrer Institute, the LHCb and Mu3e experiments reach sub-millimeter spatial resolution by using millions of thin scintillating optical fibers.

These systems demonstrate what segmented detectors can accomplish, but they also reveal a growing problem. As detectors become larger, manufacturing, assembling, and reading out millions of individual components can become a major technological and financial bottleneck.

A Radical New Approach to Particle Tracking

Researchers at ETH Zurich and EPFL are now proposing a very different strategy.

PhD student Till Dieminger, senior scientist Dr. Saúl Alonso-Monsalve, Professor Davide Sgalaberna and colleagues in his group, together with members of the Advanced Quantum Architecture Lab at EPFL in Lausanne led by Professor Edoardo Charbon, developed and tested the first prototype of a detector designed to perform ultrafast, high-resolution 3D particle imaging inside a large, unsegmented block of scintillator material.

Instead of dividing the detector into millions of tiny units, the system uses advanced camera technology to reconstruct where the light originated.

The prototype demonstration and an extensive series of simulations were described recently in Nature Communications.

Turning Light Field Photography Into a Physics Tool

The detector draws inspiration from plenoptic cameras, also known as light field cameras.

Unlike an ordinary camera, which mainly records the intensity of incoming light, a light field camera also captures information about the direction from which the light arrived. This allows it to recover depth and reconstruct a scene in three dimensions.

The technology relies on a micro-lens array (MLA) placed between the camera's main lens and imaging sensor. Each microscopic lens acts like a tiny camera, recording the same scene from a slightly different angle. When the information from all of these lenses is combined, the system can reconstruct a light field, which describes the intensity, position, and direction of the incoming light.

For particle detection, this ability is particularly useful because the light inside a scintillator may be extremely faint.

When plenoptic cameras are paired with single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) array sensors, they can detect individual photons and potentially reconstruct particle tracks even when very little light is available. Despite that promise, light field cameras had not previously been explored for particle tracking.

Inside the PLATON Prototype

The new system was developed through the PLATON project, which is funded by the Swiss National Science Foundation.

The ETHZ-EPFL team built a proof-of-concept detector that combines a micro-lens array with a SPAD imaging sensor. The sensor, known as SwissSPAD2, was developed by the EPFL team. Raytrix GmbH designed the MLA and mounted it directly onto the sensor to create the complete plenoptic imaging system.

SwissSPAD2 also provides gated photon detection. This means that the sensor records photons only within defined time windows.

That timing control helps researchers focus on periods when genuine scintillation light is most likely to be present while filtering out random background signals and other spurious counts.

Testing the Detector With Just a Few Photons

The researchers tested PLATON's spatial resolution in laboratory experiments using light levels ranging from several hundred detected photons down to only five.

They also evaluated whether the prototype could detect electrons and reconstruct their positions inside a block of plastic scintillator. The electrons were produced using a strontium-90 source.

Across the different test conditions, the simulations closely matched the laboratory measurements, giving the researchers confidence that their models accurately describe the detector's performance.

The results from the first demonstrator have already shaped the team's plans for the next version of PLATON.

Faster Timing and Greater Sensitivity

The researchers are developing a new SPAD array sensor designed to improve photon detection efficiency and provide sub-nanosecond timing for individual photons.

In the current system, photons are assigned to fixed time windows. In the upgraded version, each detected photon would receive its own precise time stamp.

That added timing information could help the system determine more accurately where each photon came from and improve the reconstruction of particle tracks.

The researchers have also optimized the plenoptic camera to expand its field of view and collect more light. Simulations presented in the paper suggest that these changes should further improve PLATON's spatial resolution.

AI Reconstructs Hidden Particle Interactions

The team also used simulations to estimate how an upgraded PLATON system could perform when detecting neutrinos.

The simulations incorporated a new image-processing method based on a neural network (NN). The system uses a Transformer architecture adapted from the type commonly used in large language models.

Rather than analyzing words, however, this Transformer examines patterns among the scintillation photons recorded by the detector. It is designed to identify correlations in where and when the photons appear, allowing it to reconstruct the original particle interaction.

The simulations indicate that an unsegmented PLATON detector with a volume of (10x10x10)cm3 could realistically achieve spatial resolution below 1mm.

They also suggest that the system could identify neutrino interactions that produce final-state low-momentum protons with both high purity and high efficiency. In other words, the detector may be able to select the desired events while rejecting many unrelated signals.

Scaling Up to a Cubic Meter

The researchers also considered how the technology might perform in a much larger detector.

Because of limited computing resources, they did not run full neutrino simulations for a one-cubic-meter block of unsegmented scintillator. Instead, they modeled a simplified point-like source of photons.

The simulations suggest that a detector of this size could achieve spatial resolution of a few millimeters, placing it on par with state-of-the-art plastic scintillator detectors.

The result is especially notable because PLATON would achieve this performance without dividing the scintillator into millions of individual pieces.

The authors believe that additional improvements to the optical design and other parts of the system could eventually make sub-millimeter resolution possible in PLATON-type detectors with volumes larger than 1m3.

Potential Uses Beyond Particle Physics

The ETH Zurich researchers believe the technology could eventually be useful far beyond neutrino experiments and particle colliders.

Because PLATON is designed to reconstruct the position of faint light signals in three dimensions, it could improve a wide range of imaging systems.

Dieminger, Alonso-Monsalve and Sgalaberna have already filed three separate patents involving the use of PLATON technology in positron emission tomography (PET). PET is a medical imaging method that tracks radioactive tracers inside the body to reveal activity in organs and tissues.

The patents cover both the scanner design and the image-processing techniques, including the NN developed by Alonso-Monsalve.

Particle physics has a long history of producing technologies that later find broader uses. The world wide web was created at CERN, while proton therapy grew from advances in particle accelerators and radiation physics.

PLATON could become another example of a physics experiment leading to a technology with major scientific and medical applications.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in particle detection using a novel camera system that simplifies and improves 3D particle tracking. This innovation has broad scientific implications for particle physics and potential large-scale benefits for medical imaging, such as enhanced PET scans, indicating a meaningful positive real-world impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New camera tracks invisible particles in 3D using light and AI
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Detecting tiny, invisible particles like neutrinos is very hard because they rarely interact with normal matter. Traditional detectors use millions of small parts to catch faint light signals from these particles, but this makes the devices complex and expensive.

**What happened** – Scientists at ETH Zurich and EPFL created a new detector called PLATON. Instead of many small parts, it uses a single block of light-sensitive material combined with a special light-field camera and AI. This camera captures not just light intensity but also its direction, allowing the detector to create detailed 3D images of particle paths. The team tested a prototype and ran simulations showing it can match or beat current detectors.

**Impact** – PLATON’s design is simpler and easier to build on a large scale because it avoids millions of tiny components. It can detect particles with very few photons and offers precise 3D tracking. This could improve experiments studying neutrinos and dark matter. Beyond physics, the technology might also make medical scans like PET scans clearer and more accurate.

**What's next step** – The researchers are improving the detector by developing better sensors to record exact photon arrival times and expanding the camera’s view. They plan to build larger versions of PLATON and test its ability to detect neutrinos in bigger volumes. They also filed patents to apply this technology to medical imaging.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new camera system using light-field technology and AI offers simpler, high-precision 3D tracking of invisible particles, with promising uses in physics and medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle caméra suit des particules invisibles en 3D grâce à la lumière et à l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il est très difficile de détecter de minuscules particules invisibles comme les neutrinos, car elles interagissent rarement avec la matière ordinaire. Les détecteurs traditionnels utilisent des millions de petites pièces pour capter les faibles signaux lumineux de ces particules, ce qui rend les appareils complexes et coûteux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’ETH Zurich et de l’EPFL ont créé un nouveau détecteur appelé PLATON. Au lieu de nombreuses petites pièces, il utilise un seul bloc de matériau sensible à la lumière combiné à une caméra spéciale à champ lumineux et à l’intelligence artificielle. Cette caméra capture non seulement l’intensité de la lumière mais aussi sa direction, ce qui permet au détecteur de créer des images 3D détaillées des trajectoires des particules. L’équipe a testé un prototype et réalisé des simulations montrant qu’il peut égaler ou dépasser les détecteurs actuels.

**Impact** – Le design de PLATON est plus simple et plus facile à construire à grande échelle car il évite des millions de petites pièces. Il peut détecter des particules avec très peu de photons et offre un suivi 3D précis. Cela pourrait améliorer les expériences d’étude des neutrinos et de la matière noire. Au-delà de la physique, cette technologie pourrait aussi rendre les examens médicaux comme les scans PET plus clairs et plus précis.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs améliorent le détecteur en développant de meilleurs capteurs pour enregistrer les temps d’arrivée exacts des photons et en élargissant le champ de vision de la caméra. Ils prévoient de construire des versions plus grandes de PLATON et de tester sa capacité à détecter les neutrinos dans des volumes plus importants. Ils ont aussi déposé des brevets pour appliquer cette technologie à l’imagerie médicale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau système de caméra utilisant la technologie à champ lumineux et l’IA offre un suivi 3D simple et précis des particules invisibles, avec des applications prometteuses en physique et en médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva cámara rastrea partículas invisibles en 3D usando luz e inteligencia artificial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Detectar partículas diminutas e invisibles como los neutrinos es muy difícil porque rara vez interactúan con la materia común. Los detectores tradicionales usan millones de piezas pequeñas para captar señales de luz débiles de estas partículas, lo que hace que los dispositivos sean complejos y costosos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de ETH Zurich y EPFL crearon un nuevo detector llamado PLATON. En lugar de muchas piezas pequeñas, usa un solo bloque de material sensible a la luz combinado con una cámara especial de campo luminoso y IA. Esta cámara captura no solo la intensidad de la luz sino también su dirección, permitiendo al detector crear imágenes 3D detalladas de las trayectorias de las partículas. El equipo probó un prototipo y realizó simulaciones que muestran que puede igualar o superar a los detectores actuales.

**Impacto** – El diseño de PLATON es más simple y fácil de construir a gran escala porque evita millones de piezas pequeñas. Puede detectar partículas con muy pocos fotones y ofrece un seguimiento 3D preciso. Esto podría mejorar experimentos que estudian neutrinos y materia oscura. Más allá de la física, esta tecnología también podría hacer que las exploraciones médicas como las tomografías PET sean más claras y precisas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están mejorando el detector desarrollando mejores sensores para registrar los tiempos exactos de llegada de los fotones y ampliando el campo de visión de la cámara. Planean construir versiones más grandes de PLATON y probar su capacidad para detectar neutrinos en volúmenes mayores. También han presentado patentes para aplicar esta tecnología a la imagen médica.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo sistema de cámara que usa tecnología de campo luminoso e IA ofrece un seguimiento 3D simple y preciso de partículas invisibles, con usos prometedores en física y medicina.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing translucent cube filled with a delicate network of tiny luminous threads and micro-lenses, gently illuminating faint, shimmering particle trails weaving through it in three dimensions, set against a warm, muted background of soft earth tones.

articles.title: NASA Pushes New Wing Design to Find Structural Limits
articles.summary: NASA researchers recently put a new wing design, appearing long and thin with a lightweight structural design, through a series of grueling tests to find its structural limits. What they found left them encouraged about the wing’s potential, even when they pushed it past its intended limits. The 15-foot Structural Wing Experiment Evaluating Truss-bracing (SWEET-15) […]
articles.content:
NASA researchers recently put a new wing design, appearing long and thin with a lightweight structural design, through a series of grueling tests to find its structural limits. What they found left them encouraged about the wing’s potential, even when they pushed it past its intended limits.

The 15-foot Structural Wing Experiment Evaluating Truss-bracing (SWEET-15) test article is part of NASA’s research to develop future ultra-efficient aircraft. The design incorporates a long wing supported by an aerodynamic strut, based on NASA’s earlier Transonic Truss‑Braced Wing concept.

The research team is working to understand whether SWEET-15’s design and its new lightweight structural designs could help commercial airliners save fuel. But first, they need to understand how it behaves under the kinds of force wings experience in flight.

Lab technicians Phil Tofts, Chris McLain, and Jeff Howell and NASA engineers Erin Anderson and Richard Larson prepare the 15-foot Structural Wing Experiment Evaluating Truss-bracing model in the Flight Loads Laboratory at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, on Thursday, Dec. 11, 2025. The model is part of NASA’s research to develop technologies for future ultra-efficient aircraft. NASA/Christopher LC Clark

The SWEET-15 design originated with combining five different advanced composite manufacturing and assembly technologies that enabled the novel structural design. The 15-foot-long test article was then designed and fabricated at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia, before traveling to NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, for testing.

Over several months, NASA engineers intentionally bent the test wing in the Flight Loads Laboratory at NASA Armstrong. Numerous strain and load sensors, including fiber-optic strain sensors, were placed throughout the structure to track how the wing responded as forces increased.

The data from the sensors confirmed the predictions made by NASA’s computer models. According to initial findings, the wing withstood the anticipated in-flight forces without issue. The results provided the team with confidence in the new manufacturing approaches and methods for connecting wing parts used in SWEET-15, which could support future efficient aircraft designs. The manufacturing approach, developed at NASA Langley used the Integrated Structural Assembly of Advanced Composites robot, aims to produce lighter and stronger composite structures for aerospace vehicles.

Lab technicians Jeff Howell, left and Chris Mount install the 15-foot Structural Wing Experiment Evaluating Truss-bracing model in the Flight Loads Lab at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, Wednesday, February 11, 2026. The model is part of NASA’s research to develop technologies for future ultra-efficient aircraft. NASA/Christopher LC Clark

The test concluded with a deliberate test-to-failure, where engineers increased loads beyond the wing’s design limits to determine how and where it would fail. The structure ultimately failed at roughly 127% of its design limit load, with visible damage appearing near the back edge of the wing and in the upper wing cover. This element of testing provided valuable insight into how the joints connecting the wing to its main strut and a secondary one, called a jury strut, behave under forces beyond the expected flight envelope.

This marks the first time a representative composite truss-braced wing configuration has undergone this type of structural evaluation. It was made possible only through NASA collaboration across centers and projects, with researchers utilizing agency resources such as the Fiber Optic Sensing System developed to gather data on both aircraft and spacecraft.

NASA research engineer Walter Hargis regulates the 15-foot Structural Wing Experiment Evaluating Truss-bracing model in the Flight Loads Laboratory at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, on Tuesday, March 31, 2026. The model is part of NASA’s research to develop technologies for future ultra-efficient aircraft. NASA/Ryan Kline

To prepare for the testing, engineers at NASA Langley designed, analyzed, and manufactured the wing and completed safety preparations and lab setup.

Researchers will now analyze the data collected during testing to inform future airframe designs and support NASA’s ongoing efforts to develop more efficient aviation technologies.

The work is being conducted through NASA’s Subsonic Flight Demonstrator project in the agency’s Research Technology Mission Directorate. The successful testing of multiple innovative components marks a milestone in NASA’s aeronautics research.

To learn more, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/aeronautics/
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's successful testing of a new lightweight, truss-braced wing design that could significantly improve fuel efficiency in future commercial aircraft. This represents a meaningful technological breakthrough with broad potential environmental and economic benefits by reducing aviation fuel consumption and emissions. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a major milestone in aeronautics research with tangible real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA tests new lightweight wing design that could boost aircraft efficiency
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on new wing designs to make future airplanes use less fuel. One idea is a long, thin wing supported by a strong but light structure called a truss, which could help planes fly better and save energy.

**What happened** – NASA tested a 15-foot model of this wing, called SWEET-15, in a special lab in California. They used sensors to see how the wing handled forces like those in real flights. The wing was pushed even beyond its expected limits until it broke, to understand its strength.

**Impact** – The wing held up well under normal forces, matching what NASA’s computer models predicted. It only failed when pushed 27% beyond its design limits, showing the design is strong and reliable. This is the first time a composite truss-braced wing has been tested this way, proving the new manufacturing methods can create lighter and stronger wings.

**What's next step** – NASA will study the test data to improve future wing designs. This research is part of a bigger project to develop ultra-efficient airplanes that use less fuel, which could change how commercial planes are built and flown.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s successful tests of a new lightweight wing design show promise for building stronger, more fuel-efficient airplanes in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA teste une nouvelle aile légère qui pourrait améliorer l'efficacité des avions
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur de nouvelles conceptions d'ailes pour rendre les avions futurs plus économes en carburant. Une idée est une aile longue et fine soutenue par une structure légère et solide appelée treillis, qui pourrait aider les avions à mieux voler et à économiser de l'énergie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La NASA a testé un modèle de 15 pieds de cette aile, appelée SWEET-15, dans un laboratoire spécial en Californie. Ils ont utilisé des capteurs pour voir comment l'aile supportait les forces comme celles en vol réel. L'aile a été poussée au-delà de ses limites prévues jusqu'à ce qu'elle casse, pour comprendre sa résistance.

**Impact** – L'aile a bien résisté aux forces normales, correspondant aux prévisions des modèles informatiques de la NASA. Elle n'a échoué que lorsqu'elle a été poussée 27 % au-delà de ses limites de conception, montrant que la conception est solide et fiable. C'est la première fois qu'une aile composite à treillis est testée de cette façon, prouvant que les nouvelles méthodes de fabrication peuvent créer des ailes plus légères et plus solides.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA étudiera les données des tests pour améliorer les futures conceptions d'ailes. Cette recherche fait partie d'un projet plus large pour développer des avions ultra-efficaces qui consomment moins de carburant, ce qui pourrait changer la façon dont les avions commerciaux sont construits et pilotés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les tests réussis de la NASA sur une nouvelle conception d'aile légère montrent un potentiel pour construire des avions plus solides et plus économes en carburant à l'avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA prueba un nuevo diseño de ala ligera que podría mejorar la eficiencia de los aviones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está desarrollando nuevos diseños de alas para que los aviones del futuro usen menos combustible. Una idea es un ala larga y delgada apoyada por una estructura ligera y fuerte llamada celosía, que podría ayudar a que los aviones vuelen mejor y ahorren energía.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA probó un modelo de 15 pies de esta ala, llamado SWEET-15, en un laboratorio especial en California. Usaron sensores para ver cómo el ala soportaba fuerzas similares a las del vuelo real. La empujaron más allá de sus límites previstos hasta que se rompió para entender su resistencia.

**Impacto** – El ala resistió bien las fuerzas normales, coincidiendo con las predicciones de los modelos por computadora de la NASA. Solo falló cuando se aplicó un 27 % más de fuerza que su límite de diseño, mostrando que el diseño es fuerte y confiable. Es la primera vez que se prueba de esta manera un ala compuesta con celosía, demostrando que los nuevos métodos de fabricación pueden crear alas más ligeras y fuertes.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA analizará los datos para mejorar futuros diseños de alas. Esta investigación forma parte de un proyecto más grande para desarrollar aviones ultraeficientes que consuman menos combustible, lo que podría cambiar cómo se construyen y operan los aviones comerciales.

**Frase clave** – Las pruebas exitosas de la NASA con un nuevo diseño de ala ligera muestran un avance para construir aviones más fuertes y eficientes en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a long, slender aircraft wing with an elegant truss-bracing structure, suspended gently in a softly lit laboratory space filled with subtle scientific instruments and glowing fiber-optic sensor lines tracing the wing’s contours, rendered in natural, muted tones to evoke innovation and careful exploration.

articles.title: Guterres: AI must be shaped by 'all of humanity', not a handful of powers
articles.summary: UN Secretary-General António Guterres on Friday urged governments and technology companies to work together to ensure artificial intelligence (AI) benefits all countries.
articles.content:
He warned that without international cooperation the technology could widen global inequality instead of advancing sustainable development.

Tweet URL

Speaking at the opening ceremony of the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (WAIC) in Shanghai, Mr. Guterres described AI as “humanity's greatest opportunity in the 21st century”, but cautioned that it could also become “one of its greatest risks”.

“Technology that will shape the future of humanity must be shaped by all of humanity”, he said, stressing that AI governance “cannot be governed by a handful of countries or companies” and that “every nation needs a seat at the table”.

Progress pending

The UN has stepped up its work on AI governance over the past year, following the adoption of the Global Digital Compact and the establishment of the UN General Assembly-backed Independent International Scientific Panel on AI – the first global scientific panel on the revolutionary technology.

The first session of the Global Dialogue on AI Governance was held in Geneva earlier this month, where governments and stakeholders convened to have open, transparent and inclusive discussions on artificial intelligence governance.

These recent initiatives are intended to help countries share expertise, promote common standards and ensure developing nations have a stronger voice in shaping the technology's future.

Mr. Guterres said the next phase must focus on turning those commitments into practical support so that all countries can benefit from AI's rapid development.

Closing the divide

The Secretary-General said AI has enormous potential to accelerate medical breakthroughs, transform education, strengthen food systems and create jobs, helping drive progress toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

But he warned that many developing countries remain at risk of being left behind.

“One-third of humanity is still offline,” he noted, while computing power, technical expertise and investment remain heavily concentrated in a small number of countries and companies.

Unless those disparities are addressed, AI could lead to “greater inequalities, greater divides in income, in opportunity, in security”.

To help bridge those gaps, Mr. Guterres said more than 20 countries, including China, have already nominated centres for a UN-supported Global Network for Exchange and Cooperation on AI Capacity Building.

He also announced that he will soon present recommendations for a Global Fund for AI and called on governments to support both initiatives.

Soundcloud

Three priorities

The Secretary-General outlined three priorities for ensuring AI benefits everyone: expanding capacity in developing countries, establishing international safety standards, and making AI more environmentally sustainable.

He said developing countries should have the tools to build AI systems using their own data, languages and expertise, while governments should adopt common approaches to testing and risk management grounded in international law.

“Human rights must be protected” the UN chief said. “Humans must keep control over every life-and-death decision.” He also stressed that “no AI system should be put in a child's hands before it has been proven safe”.

On sustainability, Mr. Guterres called on major AI companies to disclose the environmental footprint of their systems and power their operations with renewable energy by 2030, urging governments to integrate clean energy for AI into national plans.

“The defining question is whether that transformation will reduce inequalities or reinforce them,” he said. “Whether it will concentrate power or expand opportunity.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on UN Secretary-General António Guterres' call for inclusive international cooperation on AI governance to ensure AI benefits all countries, especially developing nations, and addresses risks of inequality and environmental impact. It highlights concrete initiatives and priorities that have broad global significance and potential positive impact on sustainable development, human rights, and capacity building.
articles.personality_title: UN chief calls for global cooperation to make AI fair and safe for all
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is a powerful technology that can change many parts of our lives, like medicine, education, and jobs. But if only a few countries or companies control it, AI could make the world more unequal.

**What happened** – On Friday, UN Secretary-General António Guterres spoke at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai. He urged all countries and tech companies to work together to guide AI development so everyone benefits, not just a few. He said AI’s future should be decided by all nations, not just a handful.

**Impact** – This is important because many developing countries don’t have the technology or knowledge to use AI well yet. Without help, they might fall further behind. The UN has started projects like a Global Network for AI cooperation and plans a Global Fund for AI to support these countries. Guterres also called for safety rules, respect for human rights, and efforts to reduce AI’s environmental harm.

**What’s next step** – Governments and companies will need to work on these ideas by sharing knowledge, setting safety standards, and investing in clean energy for AI. The UN will provide recommendations and support to make sure AI development is fair and helps close the gap between rich and poor nations.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is pushing for worldwide teamwork to make AI safe, fair, and useful for all countries, especially those still catching up technologically.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le chef de l’ONU appelle à une coopération mondiale pour rendre l’IA juste et sûre pour tous
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) est une technologie puissante qui peut transformer de nombreux aspects de notre vie, comme la médecine, l’éducation et l’emploi. Mais si seulement quelques pays ou entreprises la contrôlent, l’IA pourrait accroître les inégalités dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Vendredi, le secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, s’est exprimé lors de la Conférence mondiale sur l’intelligence artificielle à Shanghai. Il a appelé tous les pays et les entreprises technologiques à travailler ensemble pour orienter le développement de l’IA afin que tout le monde en profite, pas seulement une minorité. Il a déclaré que l’avenir de l’IA doit être décidé par toutes les nations, pas seulement par quelques-unes.

**Impact** – Cela est important car beaucoup de pays en développement ne disposent pas encore des technologies ou des connaissances nécessaires pour bien utiliser l’IA. Sans aide, ils risquent de prendre du retard. L’ONU a lancé des projets comme un Réseau mondial de coopération sur l’IA et prévoit un Fonds mondial pour l’IA afin de soutenir ces pays. M. Guterres a aussi demandé des règles de sécurité, le respect des droits humains et des efforts pour réduire l’impact environnemental de l’IA.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les gouvernements et les entreprises devront mettre en œuvre ces idées en partageant leurs savoirs, en établissant des normes de sécurité et en investissant dans l’énergie propre pour l’IA. L’ONU fournira des recommandations et un soutien pour garantir que le développement de l’IA soit équitable et aide à réduire les écarts entre pays riches et pauvres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ONU encourage une collaboration mondiale pour rendre l’IA sûre, juste et utile pour tous les pays, en particulier ceux qui sont encore en retard technologiquement.
articles.personality_title_es: El jefe de la ONU pide cooperación global para hacer que la IA sea justa y segura para todos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) es una tecnología poderosa que puede cambiar muchos aspectos de nuestra vida, como la medicina, la educación y el empleo. Pero si solo unos pocos países o empresas la controlan, la IA podría aumentar las desigualdades en el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – El viernes, el Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, habló en la Conferencia Mundial de Inteligencia Artificial en Shanghái. Instó a todos los países y empresas tecnológicas a trabajar juntos para guiar el desarrollo de la IA para que todos se beneficien, no solo unos pocos. Dijo que el futuro de la IA debe ser decidido por todas las naciones, no solo por unas pocas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muchos países en desarrollo aún no tienen la tecnología o el conocimiento para usar bien la IA. Sin ayuda, podrían quedarse atrás. La ONU ha iniciado proyectos como una Red Global de Cooperación en IA y planea un Fondo Global para la IA para apoyar a estos países. Guterres también pidió reglas de seguridad, respeto a los derechos humanos y esfuerzos para reducir el daño ambiental de la IA.

**Próximo paso** – Los gobiernos y las empresas deberán trabajar en estas ideas compartiendo conocimientos, estableciendo normas de seguridad e invirtiendo en energía limpia para la IA. La ONU dará recomendaciones y apoyo para asegurar que el desarrollo de la IA sea justo y ayude a cerrar la brecha entre países ricos y pobres.

**Resumen en una frase** – La ONU impulsa la colaboración mundial para que la IA sea segura, justa y beneficiosa para todos los países, especialmente para aquellos que aún están en proceso de desarrollo tecnológico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of diverse glowing hands of different sizes and colors gently holding a luminous, interconnected globe made of circuit-like patterns, surrounded by a circle of open, empty chairs symbolizing inclusive global cooperation and shared stewardship of AI’s future.

articles.title: Selectivity Emerges from Indiscriminate Photoreduction
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10897-7Selectivity Emerges from Indiscriminate Photoreduction
articles.content: Single electron transfer (SET) reduction is amongst the most fundamental strategies for the activation of organic compounds. The design of selective reactions that leverage SET is grounded by the premise that differences in substrate redox potentials predict relative rates of SET, with more favorable reductions occurring faster.1 However, across the diverse modes of redox catalysis,2,3 devising reactions that require SET to the harder-to-reduce of two reactants remains challenging. This restriction all but precludes coupling reactions when targeting substrates that are thermodynamically difficult to reduce or oxidize.4,5 Here, we introduce an alternative selectivity paradigm for outer sphere SET that is divorced from substrate redox potentials. We show that super-potent photoreductants render substrate redox potentials irrelevant through diffusion-limited SET, allowing a new selectivity profile to emerge from competition between downstream chemical steps and back electron transfer (BET). We validate these principles in the context of radical annulation reactions between cyclopropyl ketones and easier-to-reduce alkenes. While these mismatched redox potentials previously precluded such reactions, we promote selective radical annulation even as the requisite ketone reduction becomes disfavored by a volt. More broadly, these studies offer a general blueprint for the design of SET reactions that require violation of redox potential control.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in photoreduction chemistry that overcomes longstanding challenges in selective single electron transfer reactions, enabling new synthetic pathways for organic compounds. This advancement has broad implications for chemical synthesis, potentially impacting pharmaceuticals, materials science, and related fields, thus offering meaningful benefits to society.
articles.personality_title: New method improves selectivity in light-driven chemical reactions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Chemists often use single electron transfer (SET) to change organic molecules, which helps make new materials and medicines. Usually, the speed of these reactions depends on how easily the molecules accept or give electrons, called their redox potential.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered a new way to control SET reactions that does not rely on redox potentials. They used very strong light-powered chemicals called photoreductants to transfer electrons so quickly that traditional rules no longer apply. This allowed them to successfully join molecules that were previously too hard to react together.

**Impact** – This breakthrough means chemists can now create new types of chemical bonds that were difficult or impossible before. It opens up new paths for making complex molecules, which could help in developing better drugs and materials.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely explore more reactions using this method to create diverse chemical products. They may also work on making the process easier to use in labs and industry.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found a new light-driven technique that breaks old rules, enabling selective chemical reactions that were once too difficult to achieve.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode améliore la sélectivité des réactions chimiques activées par la lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chimistes utilisent souvent le transfert d’électron unique (TEU) pour modifier des molécules organiques, ce qui aide à créer de nouveaux matériaux et médicaments. Habituellement, la vitesse de ces réactions dépend de la facilité avec laquelle les molécules acceptent ou donnent des électrons, appelée potentiel redox.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert une nouvelle façon de contrôler les réactions TEU qui ne repose pas sur les potentiels redox. Ils ont utilisé des photoreducteurs très puissants, activés par la lumière, pour transférer des électrons si rapidement que les règles traditionnelles ne s’appliquent plus. Cela leur a permis d’assembler avec succès des molécules auparavant trop difficiles à faire réagir ensemble.

**Impact** – Cette avancée signifie que les chimistes peuvent désormais créer de nouveaux types de liaisons chimiques qui étaient difficiles ou impossibles auparavant. Cela ouvre de nouvelles voies pour fabriquer des molécules complexes, ce qui pourrait aider au développement de meilleurs médicaments et matériaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer davantage de réactions utilisant cette méthode pour créer des produits chimiques diversifiés. Ils pourraient aussi travailler à rendre le procédé plus facile à utiliser en laboratoire et dans l’industrie.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé une nouvelle technique activée par la lumière qui dépasse les anciennes règles, permettant des réactions chimiques sélectives auparavant trop difficiles à réaliser.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método mejora la selectividad en reacciones químicas impulsadas por luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los químicos suelen usar la transferencia de un solo electrón (TSE) para cambiar moléculas orgánicas, lo que ayuda a crear nuevos materiales y medicinas. Normalmente, la velocidad de estas reacciones depende de qué tan fácil es que las moléculas acepten o cedan electrones, llamado potencial redox.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron una nueva forma de controlar las reacciones TSE que no depende del potencial redox. Usaron productos químicos fotoreductores muy fuertes, activados por luz, para transferir electrones tan rápido que las reglas tradicionales ya no aplican. Esto les permitió unir moléculas que antes eran muy difíciles de reaccionar juntas.

**Impacto** – Este avance significa que los químicos ahora pueden crear nuevos tipos de enlaces químicos que antes eran difíciles o imposibles. Abre nuevas formas para hacer moléculas complejas, lo que podría ayudar a desarrollar mejores medicamentos y materiales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán más reacciones usando este método para crear productos químicos diversos. También podrían trabajar en hacer el proceso más fácil de usar en laboratorios e industria.

**Frase clave** – Científicos encontraron una nueva técnica impulsada por luz que rompe las reglas antiguas, permitiendo reacciones químicas selectivas que antes eran muy difíciles de lograr.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two distinct glowing orbs—one smaller and dimmer representing a challenging molecule, the other larger and brighter symbolizing an easier-to-reduce substrate—connected by dynamic, flowing streams of light illustrating rapid electron transfer; surrounding them, delicate, interlocking geometric shapes evoke the formation of new chemical bonds, all set against a softly textured, abstract background of muted natural colors.
